name: store
context: Understand pnpm's content-addressable store, virtual node_modules layout, and store maintenance commands
info: pnpm's performance and disk-efficiency come from its global content-addressable store and the virtual store inside `node_modules/.pnpm`. This node explains how packages are hard linked or symlinked into projects, what `pnpm store status`, `path`, and `prune` actually do, and why the store is the right cache target for CI and Docker. Agents need this context whenever install behavior, workspace linking, or disk cleanup matters.
body:
# pnpm Store Model

pnpm keeps package contents in a global content-addressable store and then links those contents into projects. The visible `node_modules` tree is a projection of that store, not a full copy of every package for every repo.

## Why it matters

- Disk use stays low across many repositories
- `pnpm fetch` can warm the store without building project `node_modules`
- CI caches should target the store, not random workspace directories
- Linker and hoist settings from [[pnpm/config]] change what appears in `node_modules`

sections.title: Store and virtual node_modules layout
sections.body: Packages live in the global store and are linked into `node_modules/.pnpm`, then exposed through symlinks or hoisted aliases depending on linker settings. Workspace dependencies may be linked from source or copied into the virtual store when `dependenciesMeta.*.injected` is enabled. This explains why pnpm installations are usually faster on repeat runs and why the repo-local node_modules tree can look different from npm's flattened layout.

sections.title: Store commands
sections.body: `pnpm store path` returns the active store directory. `pnpm store status` checks whether store contents changed since unpacking. `pnpm store prune` removes unreferenced packages from the store and is safe for projects, although aggressive pruning can force re-downloads later. The official docs explicitly note that pruning is best done occasionally rather than constantly, and it is blocked while a store server is running.

sections.title: Caching in CI and Docker
sections.body: Official CI examples cache the pnpm store and pair it with a lockfile key. In Docker, `pnpm fetch` can populate the store from `pnpm-lock.yaml`, then `pnpm install --offline --frozen-lockfile` uses that store without network access. This pattern is usually more reliable than caching `node_modules` directly because the store is reusable across workspaces and install modes.

links.context: Fetch and offline install workflows are store-driven
Linker and hoist settings shape what the store exposes into node_modules
Deploy pipelines often pre-warm the store before copying output
Container builds benefit from store-aware caching patterns

related.note: Install, fetch, and update commands are the main producers and consumers of store state
Caching the pnpm store is a common Docker optimization

author: dojo-community
